text:
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Emily Carter
St. Bartholomew’s Hospital
Department of Neurology, West Smithfield, London EC1A 7BE

Dear Dr Emily Carter,

Alex Turner, DOB: 14-03-1990, Hospital No: H392847 NHS No. 9845632107
Flat 12, 5 Crown Court, London EC2V 6JP

Diagnoses:

Focal epilepsy with cognitive seizure onset
MRI brain (2023) – normal
EEG (April 2024) – left temporal intermittent rhythmic delta activity with rare sharp waves

Current Medication:

Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily
Clobazam 5 mg at night (as adjunct)

Plan:

• Optimise levetiracetam to 1,000 mg twice daily if tolerated; continue clobazam 5 mg nocte
• Safety advice regarding focal seizures, including supervision near water and caution with heights
• Seizure diary to continue; next review in 6 months or sooner if change in pattern
• DVLA guidance discussed; patient currently not driving
• Support for assistance dog in training to continue; provide clinic letter for workplace and training organisation

I reviewed Alex Turner in my clinic today. We discussed an ongoing pattern of focal seizures characterised by brief impairment of thinking and awareness, described by the patient as "losing the thread" of conversation with preserved posture and no convulsions. The current frequency is focal cognitive once every two days, typically lasting 30–60 seconds, sometimes followed by mild fatigue for up to an hour. There have been no generalised tonic-clonic seizures reported.

These events are consistent with focal epilepsy, most likely of left temporal onset given the semiology and EEG findings. There are no new neurological symptoms. Sleep has improved and alcohol intake is minimal. The patient keeps an assistance dog in training, which has begun to alert family members during episodes; the patient reports this has been helpful for safety and prompting rest afterwards.

Examination today was normal. Blood tests from August 2025 showed normal full blood count, renal and liver function. There is no history of medication non-adherence. Given the persistent frequency despite current dosing, I have recommended a cautious increase of levetiracetam to 1,000 mg twice daily if well tolerated over the next two weeks, while maintaining clobazam 5 mg at night. We discussed potential adverse effects (mood irritability, somnolence) and the patient agrees to contact the service if these arise.

We reviewed standard safety measures relevant to focal seizures, including avoiding lone swimming or bathing, taking care with hot surfaces, and using a shower rather than a bath. DVLA guidance for epilepsy was discussed and the patient is aware of current restrictions on driving. I encouraged continued use of a seizure diary (including any alerts from the assistance dog) to document trends and potential triggers.

I will provide a copy of this letter to the patient’s workplace and the assistance dog training organisation to confirm the diagnosis of focal epilepsy and current management plan. I will review Alex Turner again in 6 months, or sooner if the seizure frequency increases or new features develop.

With best wishes,

entities.text: The current frequency is focal cognitive once every two days
focal cognitive once every two days

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "The current frequency is focal cognitive once every two days." This provides a clear numeric frequency. Convert "once every two days" to the allowed format: 1 per 2 day. No other seizure types with higher frequency are reported, and there are no generalized seizures. Therefore, the final normalized label is 1 per 2 day.
